text: The video demonstrates a laparoscopic D2 gastrectomy in an elderly patient. The surgeon identifies a growth near the antrum and begins a total omentectomy, starting at the mid portion of the transverse colon. The omentum is gradually divided towards the splenic flexure, freeing it on the left side. The surgeon then shifts focus to the right side, continuing the omentectomy towards the hepatic flexure. The lesser sac is opened, and attachments between the pancreas and stomach are divided.

The surgeon dissects the posterior attachments, revealing the pancreas near the head and pylorus at the D1 junction. The dissection proceeds close to the duodenum, exposing the gastrocolic vessel. The right gastroepiploic vessel is clipped and taken down using an energy source. The right gastroepiploic artery is isolated, clipped with two proximal and one distal clip, and divided using cold scissors.

The surgeon creates a small window on the lesser curvature side, carefully dissecting fibrotic adhesions while working near the gastroduodenal artery. The hepatoduodenal nodal dissection begins, exposing the common bile duct and common hepatic duct, with lymphovascular tissue being removed.

The surgeon dissects from the anterior aspect of the common hepatic duct, revealing the hepatic artery below the instrument. A bluish hue indicates the portal vein, which becomes more visible. The hepatic artery and gastroduodenal branch are clearly seen, with an anatomical variation showing early division of the right and left hepatic arteries. Both arteries run towards the liver. The right gastric artery is clipped and cut, similar to previous steps, with surrounding lymphovascular tissue visible.

The surgeon meticulously dissects around the vessels, ensuring they are completely exposed without any residual tissue. Ultrasonic shears are used with precision near the vessels. A bulky lymph node at the celiac axis is excised, leaving the vessel bare. The fatty tissue surrounding the hiatus is removed, revealing the esophagus. The duodenum is divided approximately 2.5 to 3 centimeters below, exposing the gastroduodenal artery and its pulsations, alongside the prehepatic portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC).

The surgeon holds the tissues, revealing the splenic artery beneath the instrument. The coronary vein is ligated at its origin using two proximal clips and one distal clip, then divided with cold scissors. The left gastric artery is visible, surrounded by lymphovascular tissue, which is removed. The aorta comes into view, and the surgeon carefully applies three proximal clips and one distal clip to a direct branch of the aorta, using an energy source to ensure secure ligation in this high-pressure zone.

The surgeon removes the final portion of lymphovascular tissue over the aorta, ensuring complete removal for a comprehensive DII lymphadenectomy. Dissection proceeds towards the short gastric vessels, with the tip of the pancreas visible to the right of the instrument. The spleen and stomach are clearly seen, and the entire course of the splenic artery is exposed, including the first branch of the short gastric vessels.

During the subtotal gastrectomy, the surgeon avoids splenic hilar dissection. The gastroduodenal artery, branches of the hepatic artery, and the portal vein are clearly visible. A clip is applied to the right gastric artery. The celiac axis area is shown, along with the transected duodenum and the hiatal region. The left gastric vein and artery are clipped. Fatty tissue on the lesser curvature side is dissected, leaving the area bare. The splenic short gastric vessels are identified, running towards the cranial end. A green cartridge is used for stapling the thick stomach tissue, crossing the previous staple line.

The surgeon ensures the staple line is secure and uses cold scissors to divide between the four rows of staples on the stomach. The gastrocolic vessel is clearly visible, confirming the correct surgical plane. The jejunum is measured at approximately forty centimeters from the DJ flexure, and a small enterotomy is made. A posterior gastrotomy is performed using ultrasonic shears, maintaining a distance of three centimeters from the staple line to preserve vascularity. The staple is introduced, and tissues are pulled together, ensuring the gun is closed during retrieval to prevent a wide otomy. A stay suture is placed on the inferior aspect, and suturing begins from the superior aspect, with bites overlapping one centimeter beyond the enterotomy for comprehensive closure.

The surgeon uses a PDS suture to close the enterotomies, starting from the superior aspect. Minimal mucosa is taken on the jejunum side, while a full-thickness bite is made on the stomach portion. The suturing targets an area one centimeter above the enterotomy, with an assisting instrument holding the thread for better control. The needle is precisely placed to ensure a secure anastomosis between the stomach and small bowel. A rails tube is inserted through the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon makes a small four-centimeter vertical incision in the upper midline to remove the specimen, revealing the length of the omentum and the specimen. The stomach is held manually to avoid damage, especially near the tumor. The jejunojejunostomy is performed extracorporeally.
